_attribution: LA FORJA team (Manuel Peña Zúñiga). Original item authored by the team.
stem: A fair six-sided die is rolled twice in a row. What is the probability of getting at least one 5?
author_rationale: The author marked 1/3 by adding the probabilities of each roll: 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3. That reasoning is INCORRECT: the addition rule applies only to mutually exclusive events, and here both rolls can show a 5 at the same time, so the case (5,5) is counted twice.

intended_defect.description: The marked key (1/3) double-counts the outcome (5,5). The stem is NOT ambiguous: 'at least one 5' is explicit. The error is purely one of calculation.
intended_defect.expected_finding: The bounded solver must compute, via the complement, P(at least one 5) = 1 - (5/6)² = 1 - 25/36 = 11/36, contradicting the marked key. Note that 11/36 ≠ 12/36 = 1/3, and the difference is exactly the case (5,5) counted twice.

source.excerpt: The rule P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) holds only when A and B are mutually exclusive. In the general case P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A ∩ B). For repeated independent events the complement is usually simpler: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none).
source.relevance: Identifies exactly the step the author omitted: subtracting P(A ∩ B), that is, the case in which both rolls show a 5.
